title: Building a fermentation vat for woad
subtitle: A traditional cold fermentation method using fresh Isatis tinctoria leaves, wheat bran, and slaked lime for a zero-chemical blue dye vat
excerpt: Woad (Isatis tinctoria) was the primary source of blue dye across Britain and Northern Europe before indigo imports. The traditional fermentation vat uses microbial reduction rather than chemical reduction agents: fresh leaves are composted in warm water with wheat bran and slaked lime. The resulting blue is less saturated than a hydrosulphite indigo vat but is produced without synthetic chemicals and is historically accurate.
sourceNotes: Pellew, Charles Livingston. Dyes and Dyeing. New York, 1913. Public domain. Internet Archive. Hummel, J. J. The Dyeing of Textile Fabrics. London, 1885. Public domain. Internet Archive. Both sources document woad and the traditional fermentation vat method used in British and Northern European blue dyeing prior to the synthetic indigo era.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dye vat in which reduction is achieved by microbial activity rather than added chemicals. Plant material is submerged in warm water with alkaline and nutrient additions; bacteria naturally present in the plant material and environment consume dissolved oxygen, creating the reducing conditions needed to dissolve insoluble blue dye pigment into a soluble yellow-green form that can bond to fibre.

In vat dyeing, the chemical process that converts insoluble indigo or woad pigment (blue) into its soluble form (yellow-green, called leuco-indigo). Reduction removes oxygen from the dye molecule. In a fermentation vat, bacteria perform the reduction. In a chemical vat, sodium hydrosulphite or thiourea dioxide performs the same role. Fibre dipped in a reduced vat appears yellow-green; it turns blue as it is lifted into the air and the oxygen re-oxidises the dye onto the fibre surface.

A scale measuring how acidic or alkaline a solution is, running from 0 (strongly acidic) to 14 (strongly alkaline). The woad fermentation vat needs to be held at a pH of 9 to 10 throughout. Below pH 9 the bacteria slow or stop; above pH 11 the fibre may be damaged. Slaked lime adjusts the pH upward; a small addition of wheat bran can buffer the vat if it climbs too high.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots are dedicated and never return to food use. Use stainless steel or enamelled pots. Wear gloves and apron. Work with extractor running or outdoors.

content.text: Woad was the principal source of blue in Britain before indigo imports became widespread. The plant (Isatis tinctoria) contains indigotin, the same dye pigment found in indigo but at lower concentrations. The traditional method for extracting the blue used a
fermentation vat
. Fresh leaves are composted in warm water with wheat bran and slaked lime. Bacteria naturally present in the leaves and environment perform the
reduction
that makes the pigment soluble. Soluble pigment can then bond to fibre.
The fermentation vat requires patience. It takes 3 to 5 days to establish and must be kept warm (28 to 35°C) throughout. Monitoring the
pH

is essential: the vat needs to hold between pH 9 and 10. Below pH 9 the bacteria stop working. Above pH 11 the fibre is at risk. The result is a muted blue-grey rather than the bright, deep blue of a chemical indigo vat. Woad fermentation vats were used across Northern European cloth production from the early medieval period through to the 17th century.
What you need

items.name: Fresh woad leaves (Isatis tinctoria), first or second harvest
Slaked lime (calcium hydroxide, for pH adjustment)
Wool yarn or fleece to dye, dry weight
Stainless steel or food-grade plastic vat, 10 litre minimum
Heat source capable of maintaining 28 to 35°C (seedling mat or warm room)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Harvest the leaves

Use fresh woad leaves from a first or second-year plant in its leafy (vegetative) stage, before the plant bolts and flowers. Once woad flowers the dye content in the leaves drops sharply. Harvest the outer leaves and leave the growing centre intact for further harvests. Use the leaves the same day: woad oxidises quickly once cut, and leaving leaves to wilt before vat-making reduces the dye yield.
Step 2: Build the vat

Tear or coarsely chop the fresh leaves and pack them into the vat. Pour boiling water over the leaves (enough to cover by 10 cm) and leave to steep for 30 minutes. The liquid will turn greenish-yellow. Strain out the leaves and return the liquid to the vat. Add the wheat bran. Dissolve the slaked lime in a small amount of hot water and stir it gradually into the vat, checking the pH after each addition. Aim for pH 9 to 10. The vat liquid should now be a yellow-green colour and smell slightly earthy.
Step 3: Maintain the fermentation

Keep the vat at 28 to 35°C. A seedling heat mat under the vat is effective for small containers. Cover the vat loosely to retain warmth without sealing it: the bacteria need a small amount of air exchange. Stir the vat gently once or twice daily, checking the pH each time. A blue foam (called the flower of the vat) forming on the surface after day 2 or 3 indicates the reduction is proceeding. Check the colour of the liquid below the foam: it should be a clear yellow-green, not brown or grey.

If the pH drops below 9, add a small amount of dissolved slaked lime (5 g in 100 ml water) and stir gently. If the vat smells strongly of ammonia and has turned brown, the fermentation has gone too far and the vat is probably beyond recovery: the bacteria have died back. Start again with fresh leaves.
Step 4: Test the vat

After 3 to 5 days, test whether the vat is ready. Dip a small piece of pre-wetted wool into the liquid without introducing air bubbles. Hold it below the surface for 5 minutes. Lift it slowly and hold it above the vat: the wool should emerge yellow-green and then slowly turn blue as it oxidises in the air over 5 to 10 minutes. If the wool emerges colourless or brown the vat is not reduced. Wait another 24 hours before testing again.
Step 5: Dye bath

Pre-wet the wool in cool water for 30 minutes and squeeze gently. Lower the wool slowly into the vat without splashing or introducing air. Keep the wool below the surface and move it gently for 20 minutes. Lift the wool slowly and allow it to oxidise above the vat for 10 minutes, during which the yellow-green will shift to blue. Return the wool to the vat for a second immersion of 20 minutes, then oxidise again. Each cycle builds the depth of blue. Three to four cycles give a reasonable muted blue-grey on wool at this concentration.
Step 6: Rinse and dry

After the final oxidation, rinse the wool in cool water until the water runs clear. The rinse water will run blue for the first few passes; this is surface dye and is normal. Squeeze gently without wringing. Dry in shade. Woad blue is reasonably light-fast; the colour will soften slightly over time but should not wash out completely on wool if the vat was properly reduced.
Colour variations

The woad fermentation vat gives a muted blue-grey on wool after 3 to 4 dip cycles. More cycles deepen the blue, but the maximum depth of colour is limited by the low indigotin concentration in woad compared to indigo: expect a soft powder-blue to medium blue, not a deep navy. Woad blue can be overdyed with a plant yellow (weld or St John's Wort on alum-mordanted wool) to give a soft green. Overdye the blue with a madder bath for a soft grey-purple.
Common problems

items.problem: Vat surface is grey-brown with no blue foam after 3 days
items.cause: Fermentation failed to establish: possibly too cold, pH too low, or old/wilted leaves
items.fix: Ensure the vat temperature stays between 28 and 35°C throughout. Check the pH and correct to 9 to 10 with slaked lime. Use fresh leaves. If the vat smells strongly of ammonia and has turned brown, it is spent: start again.

items.problem: Wool emerges colourless rather than yellow-green after a dip
items.cause: Vat is not yet fully reduced or was disturbed with too much air stirring
items.fix: Wait another 24 hours and test again. Stir the vat gently to avoid introducing oxygen. A reduced vat should have a yellow-green liquid below the foam surface.

items.problem: Blue is patchy with lighter and darker areas
items.cause: Wool was moved too quickly through the vat surface, introducing air bubbles, or was not kept below the surface throughout the dip
items.fix: Lower the wool very slowly and keep it fully submerged throughout each dip cycle. Move it gently in horizontal strokes rather than up-and-down movements.

items.problem: Blue washes out in the first rinse
items.cause: Too few dip cycles, or the wool was rinsed straight after dipping before it had time to oxidise on the fibre.
items.fix: Allow a full 10-minute oxidation above the vat between each dip. Complete at least 3 dip-oxidation cycles. The blue is only fixed once fully oxidised on the fibre surface.

content.text: Wastewater disposal

The spent woad fermentation vat is alkaline and contains organic matter. Once the vat is exhausted, allow it to cool and then dispose of it down a foul drain only. Dilute with plenty of water as you flush. Do not dispose of the spent vat into a storm drain, watercourse, garden soakaway, or on the compost heap: the alkalinity and fermentation products can harm soil biology and aquatic invertebrates. Spent leaves can go into general waste.
